name: Eekso — Session Open
dmNotes: THE OPENER. Order: poem → welcome + character intros → recap → where we're at → the bridge. The poem is about how stories drift in the telling — one stone, one lightning strike, one misheard word decides the next hundred years; generations pass the changed story down until someone prays to it (the religion/Torm idea, never said out loud). Built like 'The Priest's Descent' (the Shipton poem) and it ENACTS its own theme: the closing couplet misquotes the Shipton ending ('Beneath the lake...' → 'Beneath the bridge...'), then the teller admits the story changed on its way to him. See if Eddy's player catches it — don't point it out. Recap covers Session 25 (the courier hulk, the bottle, IT CONSUMES, the bird, the roar). End on the exterior reveal of Eekso from the bridge, then switch to 'The Broken Foyer' when they go through the doors.

ideas.text: The doors stand a hand's width open — an invitation dressed as an accident
Ancient hinges, new wooden bar. Someone maintains this door.
The river runs INTO the fortress through grates like teeth, and you can't hear it come out

entities.name: Eekso, from the bridge
entities.note: A fortress built across the river, fused into the cave rock. The doors stand ajar.

entities.name: The roar behind
entities.note: Sounds once more at the bridge, then never follows. Unrelated to Eekso — some other beast, maybe a young dragon (grill-queue).

content.text: START RECORDING
Session Open
The Poem
It's funny how one small thing can have such a profound impact.
One rock comes loose. Downhill it rolls.
One lightning strike, , one flame —
And and an entire forest burnt.
A story rolls the same way down,
From mouth to mouth, from town to town.
Told and retold, father to son,
Then one person makes a change in the story, whether intentional or not.
Down and down and down it goes.
Forever changed. Passed from one generation to the next, the tellers lying without even knowing it. Most of the time, that's harmless. But sometimes the story walks a darker path — until one
day, someone bows their head
to something no one ever said.
Welcome back adventurers, to the dark subterranean depths of Elor. Heads up on the next few sessions (potentially). Dungeon crawl. Before we dive in lets do a quick recap.
The Recap
Last session, you fought the umber hulk on the riverbank.
got knocked out,

fed him a potion, and you brought the thing down —during the fight, it half-shifted into something else and let out a roar so loud it literally burst some eardrums. Then you did what any normal group of adventurers would do: Cumb and
cut off a claw to cook, and

and Varen cracked the skull open to look at the brain. Eddy's Divine Sense picked up something almost divine — but strange. Foreign. Then the body melted. The claw melted too ruining Cumb and Felson's dinner plans. Felson did manage to catch some of the liquid in an empty potion bottle before the river washed the rest away, and he's still got it.
Downriver you found an old shed where somebody had been living, and studying something. Carved into the desk, over and over, in Elvish:
IT CONSUMES.
And outside, a broken collar.
Then you heard weird sounds in the dark — meows, moos, someone calling for help — and a weird colorful bird walked straight up to Eddy. Eddy to a liking to it and seemed to want to keep it.
And then, from behind you: that same roar. Again. Closer.
This is where we begin.
Where We're At
- You ran — towards the entrance to this castle
The Bridge

The river drops away in front of you, white and roaring, down into the dark underneath the structure in front of you. The sound fills the whole cave. You can feel it in the bridge under your feet — a bridge of old black stone, slick with spray, crossing right over the falls.
And on the far side, filling the cave wall to wall, is a fortress.

It's built straight across the river. The towers run into the cave rock like they grew there. The walls have been patched over and over with different stone by different hands. The river runs in underneath it through big stone grates, like teeth — and here's the thing. You can't hear it come out the other side.
Behind you, the roar sounds again. Closer.
Ahead of you, at the end of the bridge, a pair of huge doors sit open.
What are you doing?
